lens.included_activities: Independent US iron foundries that repeatedly engineer and pour ductile, gray, malleable, compacted-graphite, or semisteel castings to external customer specifications and have transferable operations in the target band.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM foundries, iron and steel mills, fabricators that do not pour castings, consumer-product businesses without repeat external casting relationships, project shells, and non-control interests.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is earned per casting, pound, tooling program, lot, or machining/finishing step under recurring OEM, distributor, infrastructure, and industrial purchase orders, often with metal and energy surcharges.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes both repeat contract foundries and captive or product-led operations. The lens retains independent external casting programs because the other models are not coherent recurring outsourced services.

a.bridge: BLS measures all foundries at NAICS 331500 rather than iron foundries alone. I assign AI exposure to quoting, casting simulation support, tooling and process documentation, scheduling, quality records, maintenance knowledge, procurement, sales, and administration, and little direct language-model substitution to melting, molding, pouring, shakeout, grinding, inspection, and material handling. Iron foundries' sand-mold and finishing intensity may make the all-foundry proxy overstate exposure.
a.rationale: Foundries are 66.92% production employment and 7.33% installation, maintenance, and repair. Grinding/lapping/polishing machine operators alone are 8.22%. Office support is only 5.25%. Higher-wage engineering, estimating, quality, planning, and management workflows are exposed, but the wage-weighted opportunity remains a minority of labor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from ductile, gray, malleable, pipe, automotive, and jobbing iron foundries, including current simulation, MES, and automated inspection, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data include steel, investment, and nonferrous foundries.
Existing casting simulation and machine automation are not separated from not-yet-automated tasks.

rho.rationale: AI-assisted RFQ review, estimating, routings, work instructions, scheduling, maintenance retrieval, defect analysis, certificates, and customer communication can be implemented within five years. Legacy systems, variable scrap and sand, low-volume jobs, safety, emissions, customer approvals, and accountable metallurgical and quality release constrain implementation. Direct furnace or pouring control is excluded.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-foundry pilots measuring accepted quotes, engineering hours, schedule adherence, scrap, rework, downtime, certificate errors, and customer returns would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No US iron-foundry five-year AI implementation study was located.
Jobbing foundries may have more document complexity but less clean data than high-volume automotive foundries.

e.rationale: Many iron foundries are contract manufacturers with repeat externally funded tooling and casting programs, fitting the outsourced-service lens despite product-form revenue. Eligibility is reduced by captive OEM plants, commodity or house-product operations, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, owner dependence, and the target-band requirement. The injected n is margin-bridged rather than an observed EBITDA count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level map of captive status, external repeat-program revenue, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, certifications, permit history, and standalone management would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Public industry counts do not separate captive and independent foundries.
The machinery margin proxy may not fit energy-intensive casting businesses.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned sales or transfers among all US employer-business owners, not completed iron-foundry control transfers. I use the 22% employer intention rate as the center, bounded downward for permits, customer qualification, capital needs, and owner technical dependence and upward for an aging industrial-owner base. Intentions likely overstate completed qualifying deals.
s5.rationale: General succession evidence supports meaningful transfer flow, and qualified casting programs, tooling, workforce, and equipment can transfer as a going concern. Some owners will instead close, liquidate, or sell assets when environmental and capital burdens exceed enterprise value.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed foundry deals and closures from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, SBA records, AFS advisers, and air-permit ownership changes, combined with owner ages, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Cross-industry owner intentions are not completed transaction probabilities.
Strategic plant purchases and family transfers may not leave a standalone eligible firm.

q.rationale: Customer-specific tooling, qualification, metallurgy, and switching costs can preserve process savings. Metal and energy surcharges also isolate some inputs. OEM buyers rebid programs, negotiate annual productivity, and benchmark piece prices, so gains are shared over renewals. Low-volume, difficult, or infrastructure castings retain more than competitive automotive programs.
q.change_evidence: Long-term agreements, annual productivity clauses, surcharge formulas, bid histories, tooling ownership, and part-level contribution-margin bridges would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No public source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through.
Scrap, alloy, energy, freight, and yield changes can mask retention.

d5.bridge: BLS projects combined foundry real output, not constant-quality demand for eligible independent iron-foundry programs. I translate the broader negative 0.8% annual 2024-34 output projection into a roughly 4% five-year decline, then widen for iron-specific infrastructure and machinery exposure versus automotive lightweighting, imports, and casting substitution. Combined foundries may overstate or understate iron demand depending on nonferrous and investment-casting trends.
d5.rationale: BLS projects foundry output from 26.9 to 24.8 billion chained dollars and employment down 14.7% through 2034. Iron castings remain embedded in motor vehicles, farm and construction machinery, petroleum and industrial equipment, motors, pipe, fittings, and infrastructure, but lightweighting, fabrication, imports, and process improvement constrain volume.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year customer forecasts and backlog by end market, constant-tonnage shipments, import penetration, water-infrastructure orders, vehicle platform awards, and announced foundry closures would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The BLS series combines all foundry metals and methods.
A foundry's demand depends heavily on a small number of awarded programs and tooling life cycles.

o.rationale: The service basket produces physical castings through charge preparation, melting, mold and core making, pouring, cooling, shakeout, cleaning, finishing, testing, and accountable shipment. Software can streamline engineering exchange and ordering but cannot replace the permitted foundry operator. Limited downside reflects customer insourcing or alternate physical manufacturing routes.
o.change_evidence: OEM insourcing, qualified additive-manufacturing substitution, fabricated or forged redesigns, and autonomous distributed casting networks would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This measures operator necessity rather than labor intensity.
Part redesign and import substitution affect d5 more than operator requirement.

narrative.executive_view: Independent iron foundries often have exactly the repeat, qualification-heavy outsourced manufacturing relationships the lens seeks, but AI applies to engineering and support workflows rather than the labor-intensive foundry floor. Demand is durable yet modestly declining in aggregate, with large variation by end market and awarded program.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist RFQs, cost estimates, routings, work instructions, production schedules, defect analysis, maintenance knowledge, quality records, certificates, purchasing, and customer service. Melting, molding, pouring, shakeout, grinding, inspection, and material movement remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Tooling, qualification, metallurgy, and switching costs support retention, particularly in complex or low-volume work. OEM annual productivity demands and program rebids share benefits in high-volume segments.
narrative.firm_availability: Many jobbing and program foundries fit the external repeat-production lens, but captive plants, customer concentration, capital needs, permits, liabilities, and owner dependence narrow the transferable pool. Closure can be more rational than sale for weak assets.
narrative.demand_durability: Infrastructure, pipe, machinery, motors, vehicles, agriculture, and industrial equipment preserve a broad physical need. Aggregate foundry output is projected to decline, reflecting lightweighting, imports, alternate processes, and productivity.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are customer-program concentration, cyclical end markets, energy and scrap costs, air emissions, silica and worker safety, capital backlog, obsolete tooling, imports, closure economics, and the lack of six-digit occupation data.
narrative.principal_driver: Qualified recurring casting programs create durable switching costs and information workflows that AI can improve.
narrative.principal_weakness: Two-thirds of employment is production work, while aggregate foundry output is projected to decline and weak plants may close rather than transfer.

sources.title: Foundries - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Foundry occupation mix: 66.92% production, 7.33% installation/maintenance/repair, and 5.25% office support.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Iron Foundries
sources.supports: Official six-digit scope and examples: purchased iron is poured into molds for ductile, gray, malleable, pipe, fittings, wheels, cookware, and other castings.

sources.title: Emission Estimation Protocol for Iron and Steel Foundries
sources.supports: Foundry process sequence and accountability: scrap/material handling, melting, mold/core making, pouring, cooling, shakeout, sand recovery, cutting, grinding, finishing, and emissions reporting.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry succession proxy: 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% planned a five-year sale or transfer.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: BLS projects foundry real output from 26.9 to 24.8 billion chained 2017 dollars and employment down 14.7% over 2024-34.

sources.title: Locating and Estimating Air Emissions from Sources of Lead and Lead Compounds
sources.supports: Iron-casting end uses include motor vehicles, farm and construction machinery, petroleum equipment, electric motors, and iron and steel industry equipment.
